message.content:
Search the FormalConjectures codebase for code that could be reused for ErdŇĎs Problem 420. The problem involves:
1. `Nat.divisors` and counting divisors (ŌĄ function)
2. Factorials (`Nat.factorial`)
3. `Real.log`
4. `Tendsto` with `atTop` filter
5. Dense sets in intervals
6. `Monotone` functions
7. Floor functions (`‚Ćä¬∑‚Ćč‚āä`)

Look in FormalConjectures/Util/ and FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/ for:
- Any helper definitions similar to F(f,n) = ŌĄ((n+‚Ćäf(n)‚Ćč)!) / ŌĄ(n!)
- Any reusable divisor-counting definitions
- Any similar "dense in an interval" formulations
- Any similar ratio-of-arithmetic-functions definitions

Also check if any other Erdos problems use similar constructions (divisor ratio, factorial divisor count, dense image). Report file paths and line numbers of anything relevant.

    24‚ÜíIf $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$, define
    25‚Üí$$F(f, n) = \frac{\tau((n + \lfloor f(n) \rfloor)!)}{\tau(n!)}$$

    27‚ÜíThree questions:
    28‚Üí1. Is it true that $\lim_{n \to \infty} F((\log n)^C, n) = \infty$ for all sufficiently
    29‚Üí   large $C$?
    30‚Üí2. Is it true that $F(\log n, n)$ is everywhere dense in $(1, \infty)$?
    31‚Üí3. More generally, if $f(n) \leq \log n$ is a monotonic function with $f(n) \to \infty$,
    32‚Üí   then is $F(f, n)$ everywhere dense in $(1, \infty)$?
    33‚Üí
    34‚ÜíErdŇĎs and Graham note it is easy to show $\lim F(n^{1/2}, n) = \infty$.
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí[EGIP96] ErdŇĎs, P., Graham, R., Ivińá, A., and Pomerance, C. proved:
    37‚Üí- $\liminf F(c \log n, n) = 1$ for any $c > 0$

    40‚Üí

    42‚Üítheory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).

    52‚Üídivisors. Here $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ and $\lfloor f(n) \rfloor_+$ is the natural

    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 419
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/419](https://www.erdosproblems.com/419)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíIf $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$, then what is the set of limit
    25‚Üípoints of $\tau((n+1)!) / \tau(n!)$?
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíErdŇĎs and Graham [ErGr80] noted that any number of the shape $1 + 1/k$ for $k \geq 1$
    28‚Üíis a limit point (and thus so too is $1$), but knew of no others.
    29‚Üí
    30‚ÜíSawhney (and independently ErdŇĎs‚ÄďGraham‚ÄďIvińá‚ÄďPomerance [EGIP96]) proved that
    31‚Üíthese are the only limit points: the set of limit points of the ratio is
    32‚Üíexactly $\{1 + 1/k : k \geq 1\} \cup \{1\}$.
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí[ErGr80] ErdŇĎs, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial
    35‚Üínumber theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Math√©matique (1980).
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí[EGIP96] ErdŇĎs, P., Graham, R., Ivińá, A., and Pomerance, C., _On the number of
    38‚Üídivisors of n!_. Analytic Number Theory (Proceedings of a Conference in Honor of
    39‚ÜíHeini Halberstam) (1996).
    40‚Üí-/
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üíopen Filter
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üíopen scoped Topology
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üínamespace Erdos419
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí/-- The ratio $\tau((n+1)!) / \tau(n!)$ as a real number, where $\tau$ counts divisors. -/
    49‚Üínoncomputable def erdos419_ratio (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    50‚Üí  ((Nat.divisors (n + 1).factorial).card : ‚ĄĚ) / ((Nat.divisors n.factorial).card : ‚ĄĚ)
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/--
    53‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 419 [ErGr80, p.83] [EGIP96]:
    54‚Üí
    55‚ÜíThe set of limit points of $\tau((n+1)!) / \tau(n!)$ is exactly
    56‚Üí$\{1 + 1/k : k \geq 1\} \cup \{1\}$, where $\tau(n) = |\text{divisors of } n|$.
    57‚Üí
    58‚ÜíA value $c$ is a cluster point of this sequence if and only if
    59‚Üí$c = 1$ or $c = 1 + 1/k$ for some positive integer $k$.
    60‚Üí-/
    61‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    62‚Üítheorem erdos_419 :
    63‚Üí    {c : ‚ĄĚ | MapClusterPt c atTop erdos419_ratio} =
    64‚Üí      answer({1} ‚ą™ {c | ‚ąÉ k : ‚Ąē, 0 < k ‚ąß c = 1 + 1 / (k : ‚ĄĚ)}) := by
    65‚Üí  sorry
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üíend Erdos419

    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 426
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíLet $f(n)$ be the maximum number of unique subgraphs in a graph on $n$ vertices.
    23‚ÜíBradańć and Christoph proved that $f(n) = o(2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!)$.
    24‚Üí
    25‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/426](https://www.erdosproblems.com/426)
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí[EnEr72] ErdŇĎs, P. and Entringer, R. C. (1972).
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí[Br75] Brouwer, A. E. (1975).
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí[BrCh24] Bradańć, D. and Christoph, M. (2024).
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üíopen SimpleGraph
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üínamespace Erdos426
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí/--
    39‚ÜíTwo subgraphs of a graph $G$ are isomorphic if there exists a graph isomorphism
    40‚Üíbetween their coerced graphs (viewed as simple graphs on their respective
    41‚Üívertex subtypes).
    42‚Üí-/
    43‚Üídef SubgraphIsomorphic {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V}
    44‚Üí    (H‚āĀ H‚āā : G.Subgraph) : Prop :=
    45‚Üí  Nonempty (H‚āĀ.coe ‚ČÉg H‚āā.coe)
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí/--
    48‚ÜíA subgraph $H$ of $G$ is a "unique subgraph" if it is the only subgraph of $G$
    49‚Üíin its isomorphism class: every subgraph $H'$ of $G$ isomorphic to $H$ must equal $H$.
    50‚Üí-/
    51‚Üídef IsUniqueSubgraph {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V}
    52‚Üí    (H : G.Subgraph) : Prop :=
    53‚Üí  ‚ąÄ H' : G.Subgraph, SubgraphIsomorphic H H' ‚Üí H' = H
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí/--
    56‚ÜíThe number of unique subgraphs of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices.
    57‚Üí-/
    58‚Üínoncomputable def numUniqueSubgraphs {n : ‚Ąē} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : ‚Ąē :=
    59‚Üí  Set.ncard {H : G.Subgraph | IsUniqueSubgraph H}
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí/--
    62‚Üí$f(n)$ is the maximum number of unique subgraphs over all graphs on $n$ vertices.
    63‚Üí-/
    64‚Üínoncomputable def maxUniqueSubgraphs (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    65‚Üí  sSup {k : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÉ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), numUniqueSubgraphs G = k}
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üí/--
    68‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 426 (Disproved):
    69‚Üí
    70‚ÜíWe say $H$ is a unique subgraph of $G$ if there is exactly one way to find $H$ as a
    71‚Üísubgraph (not necessarily induced) of $G$. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum number of
    72‚Üídistinct unique subgraphs in a graph on $n$ vertices.
    73‚Üí
    74‚ÜíA problem of ErdŇĎs and Entringer [EnEr72], who constructed a graph with
    75‚Üí$\gg 2^{\binom{n}{2} - O(n^{3/2+o(1)})}$ many unique subgraphs. This was improved by
    76‚ÜíBrouwer [Br75], who constructed a graph with $\gg 2^{\binom{n}{2} - O(n)} / n!$ many
    77‚Üíunique subgraphs.
    78‚Üí
    79‚ÜíNote that there are approximately $2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!$ non-isomorphic graphs on $n$
    80‚Üívertices, so the bound in the problem statement is trivially best possible.
    81‚Üí
    82‚ÜíErdŇĎs believed Brouwer's construction was essentially best possible, but
    83‚ÜíSpencer suggested that $\gg 2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!$ might be achievable. ErdŇĎs offered
    84‚Üí100 dollars for such a construction and 25 dollars for a disproof.
    85‚Üí
    86‚ÜíBradańć and Christoph [BrCh24] proved the answer is no:
    87‚Üí$$f(n) = o(2^{\binom{n}{2}} / n!).$$
    88‚ÜíQuantitatively, the $o(1)$ factor can be taken to be $O(\log \log \log n / \log \log n)$.
    89‚Üí-/
    90‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    91‚Üítheorem erdos_426 :
    92‚Üí    Filter.Tendsto
    93‚Üí      (fun n : ‚Ąē =>
    94‚Üí        (maxUniqueSubgraphs n : ‚ĄĚ) /
    95‚Üí        ((2 : ‚ĄĚ) ^ (n.choose 2) / (Nat.factorial n : ‚ĄĚ)))
    96‚Üí      Filter.atTop
    97‚Üí      (nhds 0) := by
    98‚Üí  sorry
    99‚Üí
   100‚Üíend Erdos426
   101‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 964
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/964](https://www.erdosproblems.com/964)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíLet $\tau(n)$ count the number of divisors of $n$. Is the sequence
    25‚Üí$\tau(n+1)/\tau(n)$ everywhere dense in $(0,\infty)$?
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíThis has been proved unconditionally by Eberhard [Eb25], who in fact showed
    28‚Üíthat every positive rational can be written as such a ratio.
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí[Er86b] ErdŇĎs, P., original problem statement.
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí[Eb25] Eberhard, S., proof that the sequence is dense.
    33‚Üí-/
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üínamespace Erdos964
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí/--
    38‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 964 [Er86b]:
    39‚Üí
    40‚ÜíThe set $\{\tau(n+1)/\tau(n) : n \geq 1\}$ is dense in $(0, \infty)$.
    41‚Üí
    42‚ÜíFor every positive real $r$ and every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a natural number $n \geq 1$
    43‚Üísuch that $|\tau(n+1)/\tau(n) - r| < \varepsilon$.
    44‚Üí
    45‚ÜíProved by Eberhard [Eb25].
    46‚Üí-/
    47‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    48‚Üítheorem erdos_964 : answer(True) ‚ÜĒ
    49‚Üí    ‚ąÄ r : ‚ĄĚ, r > 0 ‚Üí ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, őĶ > 0 ‚Üí
    50‚Üí    ‚ąÉ n : ‚Ąē, n ‚Č• 1 ‚ąß
    51‚Üí      |((Nat.divisors (n + 1)).card : ‚ĄĚ) / ((Nat.divisors n).card : ‚ĄĚ) - r| < őĶ := by
    52‚Üí  sorry
    53‚Üí
    54‚Üíend Erdos964

    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 550
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/550](https://www.erdosproblems.com/550)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíLet $m_1 \le \cdots \le m_k$ and $n$ be sufficiently large. If $T$ is a tree on $n$ vertices
    25‚Üíand $G$ is the complete multipartite graph with vertex class sizes $m_1, \ldots, m_k$, then
    26‚Üí$$R(T, G) \le (\chi(G) - 1)(R(T, K_{m_1,m_2}) - 1) + m_1.$$
    27‚Üí
    28‚ÜíFor a complete $k$-partite graph with $k \ge 2$ non-empty parts, $\chi(G) = k$, so the
    29‚Üíbound becomes $(k - 1)(R(T, K_{m_1,m_2}) - 1) + m_1$.
    30‚Üí
    31‚ÜíChv√°tal [Ch77] proved that $R(T, K_m) = (m - 1)(n - 1) + 1$.
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí[EFRS85] ErdŇĎs, P., Faudree, R., Rousseau, C., and Schelp, R.,
    34‚Üí*Multipartite graph‚ÄĒsparse graph Ramsey numbers*, Combinatorica **5** (1985), 311‚Äď318.
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí[Ch77] Chv√°tal, V., *Tree-complete graph Ramsey numbers*,
    37‚ÜíJ. Graph Theory **1** (1977), 93.
    38‚Üí-/
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üíopen SimpleGraph
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üínamespace Erdos550
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí/-- A graph $H$ contains a copy of graph $G$ (as a subgraph) if there is an injective
    45‚Üífunction from $V(G)$ to $V(H)$ that preserves adjacency. -/
    46‚Üídef ContainsSubgraphCopy {V W : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (H : SimpleGraph W) : Prop :=
    47‚Üí  ‚ąÉ f : V ‚Üí W, Function.Injective f ‚ąß ‚ąÄ u v, G.Adj u v ‚Üí H.Adj (f u) (f v)
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí/-- The off-diagonal Ramsey number $R(G_1, G_2)$: the minimum $N$ such that every
    50‚Üígraph $H$ on $N$ vertices contains a copy of $G_1$ or its complement contains a
    51‚Üícopy of $G_2$. -/
    52‚Üínoncomputable def offDiagRamseyNumber {V‚āĀ V‚āā : Type*}
    53‚Üí    (G‚āĀ : SimpleGraph V‚āĀ) (G‚āā : SimpleGraph V‚āā) : ‚Ąē :=
    54‚Üí  sInf {N : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÄ (H : SimpleGraph (Fin N)),
    55‚Üí    ContainsSubgraphCopy G‚āĀ H ‚ą® ContainsSubgraphCopy G‚āā HŠ∂ú}
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üí/-- The complete multipartite graph with vertex class sizes given by `sizes`.
    58‚ÜíVertices are pairs $(i, j)$ where $i$ indexes the part and $j$ is within the part.
    59‚ÜíTwo vertices are adjacent iff they belong to different parts. -/
    60‚Üídef completeMultipartiteGraph {k : ‚Ąē} (sizes : Fin k ‚Üí ‚Ąē) :
    61‚Üí    SimpleGraph (ő£ i : Fin k, Fin (sizes i)) where
    62‚Üí  Adj v w := v.1 ‚Č† w.1
    63‚Üí  symm _ _ h := Ne.symm h
    64‚Üí  loopless _ := fun h => h rfl
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí/-- Part sizes for the complete bipartite graph $K_{m_1,m_2}$. -/
    67‚Üídef bipSizes (m‚āĀ m‚āā : ‚Ąē) : Fin 2 ‚Üí ‚Ąē
    68‚Üí  | ‚ü®0, _‚ü© => m‚āĀ
    69‚Üí  | ‚ü®_ + 1, _‚ü© => m‚āā
    70‚Üí
    71‚Üí/--
    72‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 550 [EFRS85]:
    73‚Üí
    74‚ÜíLet $m_1 \le \cdots \le m_k$ and $n$ be sufficiently large. If $T$ is a tree on $n$ vertices and
    75‚Üí$G$ is the complete multipartite graph with vertex class sizes $m_1, \ldots, m_k$, then
    76‚Üí$$R(T, G) \le (\chi(G) - 1)(R(T, K_{m_1,m_2}) - 1) + m_1.$$
    77‚Üí
    78‚ÜíFor a complete $k$-partite graph with non-empty parts, $\chi(G) = k$, so the bound
    79‚Üíbecomes $(k - 1) \cdot (R(T, K_{m_1,m_2}) - 1) + m_1$, where $K_{m_1,m_2}$ is the
    80‚Üícomplete bipartite graph with part sizes $m_1$ (= `sizes 0`) and $m_2$ (= `sizes 1`).
    81‚Üí-/
    82‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 5]
    83‚Üítheorem erdos_550 (k : ‚Ąē) (hk : k ‚Č• 2) (sizes : Fin k ‚Üí ‚Ąē)
    84‚Üí    (hsizes_pos : ‚ąÄ i, 0 < sizes i)
    85‚Üí    (hsizes_mono : Monotone sizes) :
    86‚Üí    let m‚āĀ := sizes ‚ü®0, by omega‚ü©
    87‚Üí    let m‚āā := sizes ‚ü®1, by omega‚ü©
    88‚Üí    ‚ąÉ N‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ (n : ‚Ąē), n ‚Č• N‚āÄ ‚Üí
    89‚Üí    ‚ąÄ (T : SimpleGraph (Fin n)), T.IsTree ‚Üí
    90‚Üí      offDiagRamseyNumber T (completeMultipartiteGraph sizes) ‚Č§
    91‚Üí        (k - 1) * (offDiagRamseyNumber T
    92‚Üí          (completeMultipartiteGraph (bipSizes m‚āĀ m‚āā)) - 1) + m‚āĀ := by
    93‚Üí  sorry
    94‚Üí
    95‚Üíend Erdos550
    96‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 391
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíLet $t(n)$ be the maximal value such that $n!$ can be written as a product
    23‚Üíof $n$ positive integers, each at least $t(n)$. ErdŇĎs conjectured that
    24‚Üí$t(n)/n \to 1/e$.
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/391](https://www.erdosproblems.com/391)
    27‚Üí-/
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üíopen BigOperators Filter
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üínamespace Erdos391
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí/--
    34‚ÜíA valid representation of $n!$ as a product of $n$ positive integers in
    35‚Üínon-decreasing order.
    36‚Üí-/
    37‚Üídef IsFactorialRepr (n : ‚Ąē) (f : Fin n ‚Üí ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    38‚Üí  (‚ąÄ i, 0 < f i) ‚ąß Monotone f ‚ąß ‚ąŹ i, f i = n.factorial
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí/--
    41‚Üí$t(n)$ is the maximal value of the smallest factor in any representation of $n!$
    42‚Üías a product of $n$ positive integers in non-decreasing order.
    43‚Üí-/
    44‚Üínoncomputable def erdos391_t (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    45‚Üí  sSup {k : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÉ f : Fin n ‚Üí ‚Ąē, IsFactorialRepr n f ‚ąß ‚ąÄ i, k ‚Č§ f i}
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí/--
    48‚ÜíLet $t(n)$ be maximal such that $n! = a_1 \cdots a_n$ with
    49‚Üí$t(n) = a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_n$.
    50‚ÜíThe limit of $t(n)/n$ as $n \to \infty$ equals $1/e$.
    51‚Üí-/
    52‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    53‚Üítheorem erdos_391 :
    54‚Üí    Tendsto (fun n : ‚Ąē => (erdos391_t n : ‚ĄĚ) / (n : ‚ĄĚ))
    55‚Üí      atTop (nhds (1 / Real.exp 1)) := by
    56‚Üí  sorry
    57‚Üí
    58‚Üí/--
    59‚ÜíThere exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $t(n)/n \leq 1/e - c/\log(n)$ for
    60‚Üíinfinitely many $n$.
    61‚Üí-/
    62‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    63‚Üítheorem erdos_391.variants.rate_of_convergence :
    64‚Üí    ‚ąÉ c : ‚ĄĚ, c > 0 ‚ąß ‚ąÄ N : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÉ n : ‚Ąē, N ‚Č§ n ‚ąß
    65‚Üí      (erdos391_t n : ‚ĄĚ) / (n : ‚ĄĚ) ‚Č§ 1 / Real.exp 1 - c / Real.log (n : ‚ĄĚ) := by
    66‚Üí  sorry
    67‚Üí
    68‚Üíend Erdos391
    69‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 497
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíHow many antichains are there in the power set of an n-element set?
    23‚Üí
    24‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/497](https://www.erdosproblems.com/497)
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí[Kl69] Kleitman, D., *On Dedekind's Problem: The Number of Monotone Boolean Functions*.
    27‚ÜíProc. Amer. Math. Soc. 21 (1969), 677-682.
    28‚Üí-/
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üíopen Finset Filter
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üínamespace Erdos497
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üíopen Classical in
    35‚Üí/--
    36‚ÜíThe number of antichains in the power set of $\operatorname{Fin} n$ (the Dedekind number $D(n)$).
    37‚ÜíAn antichain is a family $F$ of subsets of $[n]$ such that for all $A, B \in F$,
    38‚Üí$A \neq B$ implies $A \not\subset B$.
    39‚Üí-/
    40‚Üínoncomputable def dedekindNumber (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    41‚Üí  Fintype.card {F : Finset (Finset (Fin n)) // IsAntichain (¬∑ ‚äÜ ¬∑) (F : Set (Finset (Fin n)))}
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí/--
    44‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 497 (Dedekind's Problem, resolved by Kleitman [Kl69]):
    45‚ÜíHow many antichains in $[n]$ are there? That is, how many families of subsets
    46‚Üíof $[n]$ are there such that no member is a subset of another?
    47‚Üí
    48‚ÜíSperner's theorem states that any single antichain has size at most
    49‚Üí$\binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$. Kleitman proved that the total number of antichains in the
    50‚Üípower set of $[n]$ is $2^{(1+o(1)) \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}}$.
    51‚Üí
    52‚ÜíEquivalently, $\log_2(D(n)) / \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.
    53‚Üí-/
    54‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 5 6]
    55‚Üítheorem erdos_497 :
    56‚Üí    Tendsto
    57‚Üí      (fun n : ‚Ąē => Real.log (dedekindNumber n : ‚ĄĚ) / (Real.log 2 * (n.choose (n / 2) : ‚ĄĚ)))
    58‚Üí      atTop (nhds 1) := by
    59‚Üí  sorry
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üíend Erdos497

    17‚Üíimport Lean.Elab
    18‚Üíimport Lean.Meta.Basic
    19‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.Answer.Syntax
    20‚Üíimport Batteries.Lean.Expr
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí/-!
    23‚Üí# The `answer( )` elaborator
    24‚Üí
    25‚ÜíThis file provides syntax for marking up answers in a problem statement.
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíNote: certain problems also providing an answer, and can be formalised
    28‚Üíusing `answer(sorry)` as a placeholder. While providing a proof simply requires
    29‚Üífinding any way to replace `:= sorry`, providing an answer is not just finding
    30‚Üíany way to replace answer(sorry): it requires evaluation of mathematical meaning,
    31‚Üíwhich is a job for human mathematicians, not Lean alone.
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üínamespace Google
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üíopen Lean Elab Term
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí/-- Indicates where the answer is in a problem statement. -/
    38‚Üí@[term_elab answer]
    39‚Üídef answerElab : TermElab := fun stx expectedType? => do
    40‚Üí  match stx with
    41‚Üí  | `(answer($a:term)) => do
    42‚Üí    mkSaveInfoAnnotation <$> postponeElabTerm (‚Üź `(by exact $a)) expectedType?
    43‚Üí  | _ => Elab.throwUnsupportedSyntax
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üíopen InfoTree
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí/-- An answer: a term, and the context in which it was elaborated -/
    48‚Üístructure AnswerInfo where
    49‚Üí  ctx : Elab.ContextInfo
    50‚Üí  term : Elab.TermInfo
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/-- Print an answer -/
    53‚Üídef AnswerInfo.format (a : AnswerInfo) : Elab.Term.TermElabM MessageData :=
    54‚Üí  Meta.withMCtx a.ctx.mctx <| Meta.withLCtx a.term.lctx {} do
    55‚Üí    let t ‚Üź Meta.inferType a.term.expr
    56‚Üí    let m ‚Üź Meta.mkFreshExprMVar t
    57‚Üí    addMessageContextFull m!"{a.term.expr} in context:{indentD m.mvarId!}"
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí/-- Find answers by inspecting an `InfoTree` -/
    60‚Üípartial def getAnswers {m} [Monad m] (i : InfoTree) : m (Array AnswerInfo) :=
    61‚Üí  go none i
    62‚Üíwhere go : _ ‚Üí InfoTree ‚Üí _
    63‚Üí  | ctx?, context ctx t => go (ctx.mergeIntoOuter? ctx?) t
    64‚Üí  | some ctx, node i cs => do
    65‚Üí    let ctx := i.updateContext? ctx
    66‚Üí    if let .ofTermInfo t := i then
    67‚Üí      if t.elaborator == ``answerElab then
    68‚Üí        if let .some ctx := ctx then
    69‚Üí          return #[‚ü®ctx, t‚ü©]
    70‚Üí    return (‚Üź cs.mapM (go <| i.updateContext? ctx)).toArray.flatten
    71‚Üí  | _, _ => pure #[]
    72‚Üí
    73‚Üíend Google

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 256
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/256](https://www.erdosproblems.com/256)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíLet $n \geq 1$ and $f(n)$ be maximal such that for any integers
    25‚Üí$1 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_n$,
    26‚Üí$$\max_{|z|=1} \left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right| \geq f(n).$$
    27‚Üí
    28‚ÜíEquivalently, $f(n)$ is the infimum over all non-decreasing sequences of positive
    29‚Üíintegers $(a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ of the supremum of $\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$ over the
    30‚Üíunit circle.
    31‚Üí
    32‚ÜíErdŇĎs conjectured that there exists $c > 0$ such that $\log f(n) \gg n^c$ [Er61, Er64b].
    33‚Üí
    34‚ÜíThis specific conjecture was answered negatively by Belov and Konyagin [BeKo96],
    35‚Üíwho proved that $\log f(n) \ll (\log n)^4$. The broader problem of precisely
    36‚Üíestimating $f(n)$ remains open.
    37‚Üí-/
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üíopen Complex BigOperators Finset
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üínamespace Erdos256
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí/-- The supremum of $\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$ as $z$ ranges over the unit circle,
    44‚Üífor a fixed sequence of exponents $a$. -/
    45‚Üínoncomputable def unitCircleMaxProd (n : ‚Ąē) (a : Fin n ‚Üí ‚Ąē) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    46‚Üí  sSup {y : ‚ĄĚ | ‚ąÉ z : ‚Ąā, ‚ÄĖz‚ÄĖ = 1 ‚ąß
    47‚Üí    y = ‚ÄĖ‚ąŹ i : Fin n, (1 - z ^ (a i))‚ÄĖ}
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí/-- $f(n)$ is the largest real number such that for any positive integers
    50‚Üí$1 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_n$, the maximum of $\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$
    51‚Üíon the unit circle is at least $f(n)$. Equivalently, the infimum of
    52‚Üí`unitCircleMaxProd` over all non-decreasing sequences of positive integers of
    53‚Üílength $n$. -/
    54‚Üínoncomputable def erdos256_f (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    55‚Üí  sInf {y : ‚ĄĚ | ‚ąÉ (a : Fin n ‚Üí ‚Ąē),
    56‚Üí    (‚ąÄ i, 0 < a i) ‚ąß Monotone a ‚ąß
    57‚Üí    y = unitCircleMaxProd n a}
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí/--
    60‚ÜíErdŇĎs Conjecture (Problem 256) [Er61, Er64b]:
    61‚Üí
    62‚ÜíDoes there exist a constant $c > 0$ such that $\log f(n) \gg n^c$, i.e., do there exist
    63‚Üíconstants $c > 0$ and $C > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$,
    64‚Üí$$\log f(n) \geq C \cdot n^c?$$
    65‚Üí
    66‚ÜíThis was answered negatively by Belov and Konyagin [BeKo96], who showed
    67‚Üí$\log f(n) \ll (\log n)^4$.
    68‚Üí-/
    69‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11 30]
    70‚Üítheorem erdos_256 : answer(False) ‚ÜĒ
    71‚Üí    ‚ąÉ c : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < c ‚ąß
    72‚Üí    ‚ąÉ C : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < C ‚ąß
    73‚Üí    ‚ąÉ N‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, N‚āÄ ‚Č§ n ‚Üí
    74‚Üí      Real.log (erdos256_f n) ‚Č• C * (n : ‚ĄĚ) ^ c := by
    75‚Üí  sorry
    76‚Üí
    77‚Üíend Erdos256

    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 464
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíFor any lacunary sequence of natural numbers (where the ratio of consecutive terms is bounded
    23‚Üíaway from 1), there exists an irrational number őł such that the sequence of distances from
    24‚Üíőł¬∑n‚āĖ to the nearest integer is not dense in [0, 1/2].
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/464](https://www.erdosproblems.com/464)

    31‚Üí/-- The distance of a real number from the nearest integer. -/
    32‚Üínoncomputable def distNearestInt (x : ‚ĄĚ) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    33‚Üí  min (Int.fract x) (1 - Int.fract x)
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí/--
    36‚ÜíA sequence $a : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ is lacunary if it is strictly increasing and there
    37‚Üíexists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $a(k+1) \geq (1+\varepsilon) \cdot a(k)$ for all $k$.
    38‚Üí-/
    39‚Üídef IsLacunary (a : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    40‚Üí  StrictMono a ‚ąß ‚ąÉ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, őĶ > 0 ‚ąß ‚ąÄ k : ‚Ąē, (a (k + 1) : ‚ĄĚ) ‚Č• (1 + őĶ) * (a k : ‚ĄĚ)
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí/--

    44‚Üí
    45‚ÜíLet $A = \{n_1 < n_2 < \cdots\} \subset \mathbb{N}$ be a lacunary sequence (there exists
    46‚Üí$\varepsilon > 0$ with $n_{k+1} \geq (1+\varepsilon)n_k$ for all $k$). Then there exists an
    47‚Üíirrational $\theta$ such that $\{\| \theta n_k \| : k \geq 1\}$ is not dense in $[0, 1/2]$,
    48‚Üíwhere $\|x\|$ denotes the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer.
    49‚Üí
    50‚ÜíThe "not dense" condition is formalized as: there exist $0 \leq c < d \leq 1/2$ such that
    51‚Üí$\|\theta n_k\| \notin (c, d)$ for all $k$, i.e., the image avoids some open subinterval
    52‚Üíof $[0, 1/2]$.
    53‚Üí-/
    54‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    55‚Üítheorem erdos_464 :
    56‚Üí    ‚ąÄ (a : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē), IsLacunary a ‚Üí
    57‚Üí    ‚ąÉ őł : ‚ĄĚ, Irrational őł ‚ąß
    58‚Üí      ‚ąÉ c d : ‚ĄĚ, 0 ‚Č§ c ‚ąß c < d ‚ąß d ‚Č§ 1 / 2 ‚ąß
    59‚Üí        ‚ąÄ k : ‚Ąē, distNearestInt (őł * ‚ÜĎ(a k)) ‚ąČ Set.Ioo c d := by

    62‚Üíend Erdos464

    15‚Üí-/
    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 381
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/381](https://www.erdosproblems.com/381)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíIs it true that $Q(x) \gg_k (\log x)^k$ for every $k \geq 1$, where $Q(x)$
    25‚Üícounts the number of highly composite numbers in $[1, x]$? Disproved by
    26‚ÜíNicolas [Ni71].
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí[Er44] ErdŇĎs, P., _On highly composite numbers_,
    29‚ÜíJ. London Math. Soc. 19 (1944), 130‚Äď133.
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí[Ni71] Nicolas, J.-L., _R√©partition des nombres hautement compos√©s
    32‚Üíde Ramanujan_, Canad. J. Math. 23 (1971), 116‚Äď130.
    33‚Üí-/
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üíopen Classical Finset Real
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üínamespace Erdos381
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí/-- A natural number $n$ is highly composite if every smaller positive natural
    40‚Üínumber has strictly fewer divisors. -/
    41‚Üídef IsHighlyComposite (n : ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    42‚Üí  0 < n ‚ąß ‚ąÄ m : ‚Ąē, 0 < m ‚Üí m < n ‚Üí (Nat.divisors m).card < (Nat.divisors n).card
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí/-- `highlyCompositeCount x` counts the number of highly composite numbers in $[1, x]$. -/
    45‚Üínoncomputable def highlyCompositeCount (x : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    46‚Üí  ((Finset.range x).filter (fun n => IsHighlyComposite (n + 1))).card
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí/--
    49‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 381 (Disproved) [Er44]:
    50‚Üí
    51‚ÜíA number $n$ is highly composite if $\tau(m) < \tau(n)$ for all $m < n$, where $\tau(m)$
    52‚Üícounts the number of divisors of $m$. Let $Q(x)$ count the number of highly
    53‚Üícomposite numbers in $[1, x]$.
    54‚Üí
    55‚ÜíErdŇĎs asked whether $Q(x) \gg_k (\log x)^k$ for every $k \geq 1$.
    56‚Üí
    57‚ÜíErdŇĎs [Er44] proved $Q(x) \gg (\log x)^{1+c}$ for some constant $c > 0$.
    58‚Üí
    59‚ÜíThe answer is no: Nicolas [Ni71] showed that $Q(x)$ does not grow faster
    60‚Üíthan a fixed power of $\log x$.
    61‚Üí-/
    62‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    63‚Üítheorem erdos_381 :
    64‚Üí    answer(False) ‚ÜĒ
    65‚Üí      ‚ąÄ (k : ‚Ąē), 1 ‚Č§ k ‚Üí ‚ąÉ c : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < c ‚ąß
    66‚Üí        ‚ąÉ N‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ x : ‚Ąē, N‚āÄ ‚Č§ x ‚Üí
    67‚Üí          c * (Real.log (x : ‚ĄĚ)) ^ k ‚Č§ (highlyCompositeCount x : ‚ĄĚ) := by
    68‚Üí  sorry
    69‚Üí
    70‚Üíend Erdos381
    71‚Üí

    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 122
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/122](https://www.erdosproblems.com/122)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíFor which arithmetic functions $f$ is it true that the count of $n$ with $n + f(n)$ in a
    25‚Üíshort interval $(x, x + F(x))$, divided by $F(x)$, is unbounded? ErdŇĎs‚ÄďPomerance‚ÄďS√°rk√∂zy
    26‚Üíproved this for $f = \tau$ (divisor count) and $f = \omega$ (distinct prime factor count).
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí[Er97] ErdŇĎs, P., *Some of my new and almost new problems and results in combinatorial
    29‚Üínumber theory* (1997).
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí[Er97e] ErdŇĎs, P., *Some problems and results on combinatorial number theory* (1997).
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí[EPS97] ErdŇĎs, P., Pomerance, C., and S√°rk√∂zy, A., *On locally repeated values of certain
    34‚Üíarithmetic functions, III* (1997).
    35‚Üí-/
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üíopen scoped Classical
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üíopen Filter
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üínamespace Erdos122
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí/-- The count of natural numbers $n$ such that $n + f(n)$ falls in the open real
    44‚Üíinterval $(x, x + F(x))$. Since $n \le n + f(n) < x + F(x)$ requires $n < x + F(x)$,
    45‚Üíit suffices to search $n$ in the range $[0, x + \lceil F(x) \rceil]$. -/
    46‚Üínoncomputable def shiftCount (f : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē) (F : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ) (x : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    47‚Üí  ((Finset.range (x + ‚ĆąF x‚ĆČ‚āä + 1)).filter
    48‚Üí    (fun (n : ‚Ąē) => (x : ‚ĄĚ) < (n : ‚ĄĚ) + (f n : ‚ĄĚ) ‚ąß
    49‚Üí              (n : ‚ĄĚ) + (f n : ‚ĄĚ) < (x : ‚ĄĚ) + F x)).card
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üí/-- The natural density of a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is zero: the proportion of elements
    52‚Üíin $\{0, \ldots, N-1\}$ belonging to $A$ tends to $0$ as $N \to \infty$. -/
    53‚Üídef HasNaturalDensityZero (A : Set ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    54‚Üí  Tendsto
    55‚Üí    (fun N : ‚Ąē => ((Finset.range N).filter (¬∑ ‚ąą A)).card / (N : ‚ĄĚ))
    56‚Üí    atTop (nhds 0)
    57‚Üí
    58‚Üí/-- $f(n)/F(n) \to 0$ for almost all $n$ in the natural-density sense:
    59‚Üífor every $\varepsilon > 0$, the set $\{n : f(n)/F(n) \ge \varepsilon\}$ has natural
    60‚Üídensity zero. -/
    61‚Üídef AlmostAllRatioVanishes (f : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē) (F : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ) : Prop :=
    62‚Üí  ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < őĶ ‚Üí HasNaturalDensityZero {n : ‚Ąē | őĶ ‚Č§ (f n : ‚ĄĚ) / F n}
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí/-- The ErdŇĎs-122 property of $f$: for any positive $F$ with $f(n)/F(n) \to 0$
    65‚Üíin natural density, the ratio $\#\{n \in \mathbb{N} : n+f(n) \in (x, x+F(x))\} / F(x)$
    66‚Üíis unbounded as $x \to \infty$ (equivalently, its limsup equals $+\infty$). -/
    67‚Üídef HasErdos122Property (f : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    68‚Üí  ‚ąÄ F : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ, (‚ąÄ n, 0 < F n) ‚Üí AlmostAllRatioVanishes f F ‚Üí
    69‚Üí    ‚ąÄ C : ‚ĄĚ, ‚ąÉŠ∂† x : ‚Ąē in atTop, C < (shiftCount f F x : ‚ĄĚ) / F x
    70‚Üí
    71‚Üí/--
    72‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem #122 [Er97, Er97e, EPS97]
    73‚Üí
    74‚ÜíFor which number-theoretic functions $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ is it true that for any
    75‚Üípositive function $F : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying $f(n)/F(n) \to 0$ for almost all $n$
    76‚Üí(in the natural density sense), there are infinitely many $x$ for which
    77‚Üí$$\#\{n \in \mathbb{N} : n + f(n) \in (x, x + F(x))\} / F(x) \to \infty?$$
    78‚Üí
    79‚ÜíErdŇĎs, Pomerance, and S√°rk√∂zy [EPS97] proved that this holds when:
    80‚Üí- $f(n) = \tau(n)$ = number of divisors of $n$, and
    81‚Üí- $f(n) = \omega(n)$ = number of distinct prime divisors of $n$.
    82‚Üí
    83‚ÜíErdŇĎs believed the property to be FALSE for:
    84‚Üí- $f(n) = \varphi(n)$ (Euler's totient function), and
    85‚Üí- $f(n) = \sigma(n)$ (sum of divisors).
    86‚Üí
    87‚ÜíThe full characterization of which $f$ satisfy `HasErdos122Property` remains open.
    88‚ÜíThe theorem below records the two proved instances.
    89‚Üí-/
    90‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    91‚Üítheorem erdos_122 :
    92‚Üí    HasErdos122Property (fun n => (Nat.divisors n).card) ‚ąß
    93‚Üí    HasErdos122Property (fun n => (Nat.primeFactorsList n).toFinset.card) := by
    94‚Üí  sorry
    95‚Üí
    96‚Üíend Erdos122
    97‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 18
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/18](https://www.erdosproblems.com/18)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíWe call $m$ *practical* if every integer $1 \leq n < m$ is the sum of distinct divisors
    25‚Üíof $m$. If $m$ is practical, let $h(m)$ be the least $k$ such that every $1 \leq n < m$
    26‚Üíis the sum of at most $k$ distinct divisors of $m$.
    27‚Üí
    28‚ÜíThree questions:
    29‚Üí1. Are there infinitely many practical $m$ such that $h(m) < (\log \log m)^{O(1)}$?
    30‚Üí2. Is it true that $h(n!) < n^{o(1)}$?
    31‚Üí3. Or perhaps even $h(n!) < (\log n)^{O(1)}$?
    32‚Üí
    33‚ÜíKnown: ErdŇĎs showed $h(n!) < n$. Vose [Vo85] proved infinitely many practical $m$ with
    34‚Üí$h(m) \ll (\log m)^{1/2}$. ErdŇĎs offered \$250 for a proof or disproof of whether
    35‚Üí$h(n!) < (\log n)^{O(1)}$ [Er81h].
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí## References
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí- [Er74b], [Er79], [Er81h], [Er95], [Er96b], [Er98]: Various papers by ErdŇĎs on practical numbers.
    40‚Üí- [ErGr80] ErdŇĎs, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    41‚Üí- [Vo85] Vose, M.: Proof of a conjecture of ErdŇĎs concerning practical numbers.
    42‚Üí-/
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üíopen Real Filter
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üínamespace Erdos18
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí/-- $m$ is practical if every integer $1 \leq n < m$ can be represented as a sum
    49‚Üí    of distinct divisors of $m$. -/
    50‚Üídef IsPractical (m : ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    51‚Üí  ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, 1 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí n < m ‚Üí
    52‚Üí    ‚ąÉ S : Finset ‚Ąē, S ‚äÜ Nat.divisors m ‚ąß S.sum id = n
    53‚Üí
    54‚Üí/-- For a practical number $m$, $\mathrm{practicalH}(m)$ is the minimum $k$ such that every
    55‚Üí    integer $1 \leq n < m$ can be expressed as the sum of at most $k$ distinct
    56‚Üí    divisors of $m$. -/
    57‚Üínoncomputable def practicalH (m : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    58‚Üí  sInf {k : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, 1 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí n < m ‚Üí
    59‚Üí    ‚ąÉ S : Finset ‚Ąē, S ‚äÜ Nat.divisors m ‚ąß S.card ‚Č§ k ‚ąß S.sum id = n}
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí/--
    62‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 18 [Er74b, Er79, ErGr80, Er81h, Er95, Er96b, Er98]:
    63‚Üí
    64‚ÜíConjecture (1): There are infinitely many practical $m$ such that
    65‚Üí$h(m) < (\log \log m)^{O(1)}$, i.e., there exists a constant $C > 0$ such that
    66‚Üíinfinitely many practical $m$ satisfy $h(m) < (\log \log m)^C$.
    67‚Üí-/
    68‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    69‚Üítheorem erdos_18 :
    70‚Üí    ‚ąÉ C : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < C ‚ąß
    71‚Üí    ‚ąÄ N : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÉ m : ‚Ąē, m ‚Č• N ‚ąß IsPractical m ‚ąß
    72‚Üí      (practicalH m : ‚ĄĚ) < (Real.log (Real.log (m : ‚ĄĚ))) ^ C := by
    73‚Üí  sorry
    74‚Üí
    75‚Üí/--
    76‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 18 [Er74b, Er79, ErGr80, Er81h, Er95, Er96b, Er98]:
    77‚Üí
    78‚ÜíConjecture (2): $h(n!) < n^{o(1)}$, i.e., for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all
    79‚Üísufficiently large $n$, $h(n!) < n^\varepsilon$.
    80‚Üí-/
    81‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    82‚Üítheorem erdos_18.variants.factorial_subpolynomial :
    83‚Üí    ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < őĶ ‚Üí ‚ąÄŠ∂† n : ‚Ąē in atTop,
    84‚Üí      (practicalH n.factorial : ‚ĄĚ) < (n : ‚ĄĚ) ^ őĶ := by
    85‚Üí  sorry
    86‚Üí
    87‚Üí/--
    88‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 18 [Er74b, Er79, ErGr80, Er81h, Er95, Er96b, Er98]:
    89‚Üí
    90‚ÜíConjecture (3): $h(n!) < (\log n)^{O(1)}$, i.e., there exists a constant $C > 0$
    91‚Üísuch that for all sufficiently large $n$, $h(n!) < (\log n)^C$.
    92‚Üí
    93‚ÜíErdŇĎs offered \$250 for a proof or disproof of this statement [Er81h].
    94‚Üí-/
    95‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    96‚Üítheorem erdos_18.variants.factorial_polylog :
    97‚Üí    ‚ąÉ C : ‚ĄĚ, 0 < C ‚ąß ‚ąÄŠ∂† n : ‚Ąē in atTop,
    98‚Üí      (practicalH n.factorial : ‚ĄĚ) < (Real.log (n : ‚ĄĚ)) ^ C := by
    99‚Üí  sorry
   100‚Üí
   101‚Üíend Erdos18
   102‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1053
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1053](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1053)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíCall a number $k$-perfect if $\sigma(n) = kn$, where $\sigma(n)$ is the sum of the divisors
    25‚Üíof $n$. Must $k = o(\log \log n)$?
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíA question of ErdŇĎs, as reported in problem B2 of Guy's collection.
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí[Gu04] Guy, R., *Unsolved Problems in Number Theory*, 3rd edition, Springer, 2004.
    30‚Üí-/
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üíopen Finset Real
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üínamespace Erdos1053
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí/-- The sum-of-divisors function $\sigma(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} d$. -/
    37‚Üídef sumOfDivisors (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    38‚Üí  (Nat.divisors n).sum id
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí/-- A natural number $n$ is $k$-perfect if $n \geq 1$ and $\sigma(n) = k \cdot n$. -/
    41‚Üídef IsMultiplyPerfect (n k : ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    42‚Üí  n ‚Č• 1 ‚ąß sumOfDivisors n = k * n
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí/--
    45‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1053 [Gu04]:
    46‚Üí
    47‚ÜíIf $n$ is a $k$-perfect number ($\sigma(n) = kn$), must $k = o(\log \log n)$ as $n \to \infty$
    48‚Üíamong multiply perfect numbers?
    49‚Üí
    50‚ÜíEquivalently: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the set of multiply perfect numbers $n$
    51‚Üíwith $\sigma(n)/n \geq \varepsilon \cdot \log(\log(n))$ is finite.
    52‚Üí-/
    53‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    54‚Üítheorem erdos_1053 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    55‚Üí    ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, őĶ > 0 ‚Üí
    56‚Üí    Set.Finite {n : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÉ k : ‚Ąē, IsMultiplyPerfect n k ‚ąß
    57‚Üí      (k : ‚ĄĚ) ‚Č• őĶ * Real.log (Real.log (n : ‚ĄĚ))} := by
    58‚Üí  sorry
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üíend Erdos1053
    61‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1100
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1100](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1100)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíIf $1 = d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)} = n$ are the divisors of $n$, let $\tau^\perp(n)$ count the
    25‚Üínumber of $i$ for which $\gcd(d_i, d_{i+1}) = 1$.
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíPart 1: Is it true that $\tau^\perp(n)/\omega(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$?
    28‚Üí
    29‚ÜíPart 2: Is it true that $\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$ for all $n$?
    30‚ÜíEquivalently, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and sufficiently large $n$,
    31‚Üí$\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^\varepsilon)$.
    32‚Üí
    33‚ÜíPart 3: Let $g(k) = \max$ over squarefree $n$ with $\omega(n) = k$ of $\tau^\perp(n)$.
    34‚ÜíDetermine the growth of $g(k)$. ErdŇĎs and Simonovits proved
    35‚Üí$(\sqrt{2} + o(1))^k < g(k) < (2 - c)^k$ for some constant $c > 0$.
    36‚Üí-/
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üíopen Finset Real
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üínamespace Erdos1100
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí/-- The sorted list of divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    43‚Üídef sortedDivisors (n : ‚Ąē) : List ‚Ąē :=
    44‚Üí  (Nat.divisors n).sort (¬∑ ‚Č§ ¬∑)
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üí/-- $\tau^\perp(n)$: the number of indices $i$ such that $\gcd(d_i, d_{i+1}) = 1$,
    47‚Üíwhere $d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)}$ are the divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    48‚Üídef tauPerp (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    49‚Üí  let ds := sortedDivisors n
    50‚Üí  ((ds.zip ds.tail).filter (fun p => Nat.gcd p.1 p.2 == 1)).length
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/--
    53‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1100, Part 1:
    54‚ÜíIs it true that $\tau^\perp(n)/\omega(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$? That is, for every
    55‚Üíbound $M$, the natural density of $\{n : \tau^\perp(n) \le M \cdot \omega(n)\}$ is zero.
    56‚Üí-/
    57‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    58‚Üítheorem erdos_1100 :
    59‚Üí    answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    60‚Üí      ‚ąÄ M : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, őĶ > 0 ‚Üí
    61‚Üí        ‚ąÉ N‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ N : ‚Ąē, N ‚Č• N‚āÄ ‚Üí
    62‚Üí          (((Finset.range N).filter (fun n =>
    63‚Üí            tauPerp n ‚Č§ M * n.primeFactors.card)).card : ‚ĄĚ) / (N : ‚ĄĚ) < őĶ := by
    64‚Üí  sorry
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí/--
    67‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1100, Part 2:
    68‚ÜíIs it true that for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    69‚Üí$\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^\varepsilon)$?
    70‚ÜíThis formalizes the conjecture that $\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$.
    71‚Üí-/
    72‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    73‚Üítheorem erdos_1100.variants.part2 :
    74‚Üí    answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    75‚Üí      ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, őĶ > 0 ‚Üí
    76‚Üí        ‚ąÉ N‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, n ‚Č• N‚āÄ ‚Üí
    77‚Üí          (tauPerp n : ‚ĄĚ) < Real.exp ((Real.log (n : ‚ĄĚ)) ^ őĶ) := by
    78‚Üí  sorry
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí/--
    81‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1100, Part 3 (upper bound, proved by ErdŇĎs‚ÄďSimonovits):
    82‚ÜíThere exists $c > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $k$, every squarefree $n$
    83‚Üíwith $\omega(n) = k$ satisfies $\tau^\perp(n) < (2 - c)^k$.
    84‚Üí-/
    85‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    86‚Üítheorem erdos_1100.variants.part3_upper :
    87‚Üí    ‚ąÉ c : ‚ĄĚ, c > 0 ‚ąß
    88‚Üí      ‚ąÉ K‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ k : ‚Ąē, k ‚Č• K‚āÄ ‚Üí
    89‚Üí        ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, Squarefree n ‚Üí n.primeFactors.card = k ‚Üí
    90‚Üí          (tauPerp n : ‚ĄĚ) < (2 - c) ^ k := by
    91‚Üí  sorry
    92‚Üí
    93‚Üí/--
    94‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1100, Part 3 (lower bound, proved by ErdŇĎs‚ÄďSimonovits):
    95‚ÜíFor every $\varepsilon > 0$ and sufficiently large $k$, there exists a squarefree $n$ with
    96‚Üí$\omega(n) = k$ and $\tau^\perp(n) > (\sqrt{2} - \varepsilon)^k$.
    97‚Üí-/
    98‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    99‚Üítheorem erdos_1100.variants.part3_lower :
   100‚Üí    ‚ąÄ őĶ : ‚ĄĚ, őĶ > 0 ‚Üí
   101‚Üí      ‚ąÉ K‚āÄ : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ k : ‚Ąē, k ‚Č• K‚āÄ ‚Üí
   102‚Üí        ‚ąÉ n : ‚Ąē, Squarefree n ‚ąß n.primeFactors.card = k ‚ąß
   103‚Üí          (tauPerp n : ‚ĄĚ) > ((2 : ‚ĄĚ) ^ ((1 : ‚ĄĚ) / 2) - őĶ) ^ k := by
   104‚Üí  sorry
   105‚Üí
   106‚Üíend Erdos1100
   107‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 859
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/859](https://www.erdosproblems.com/859)
    23‚Üí-/
    24‚Üí
    25‚Üínamespace Erdos859
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí/--
    28‚Üí`DivisorSumSet t` is the set of natural numbers `n` such that `t` can be represented as
    29‚Üía sum of distinct divisors of `n`.
    30‚Üí-/
    31‚Üídef DivisorSumSet (t : ‚Ąē) := { n : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÉ s ‚äÜ Nat.divisors n, t = ‚ąĎ i ‚ąą s, i }
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üíopen Asymptotics Filter
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí/-- A weaker version of the problem proved by Erdos:
    36‚ÜíThe density `d‚āú` of `DivisorSumSet (t : ‚Ąē)` is bounded from below by `1 / log (t) ^ c‚āÉ` and
    37‚Üífrom above by `1 / log (t) ^ c‚āĄ` for some positive constants `c‚āÉ` and `c‚āĄ`.
    38‚Üí-/
    39‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    40‚Üítheorem erdos_859.variants.erdos_upper_lower_bounds : ‚ąÉŠĶČ (c‚āÉ > (0 : ‚ĄĚ)) (c‚āĄ > (0 : ‚ĄĚ)) (t‚āÄ : ‚Ąē),
    41‚Üí    ‚ąÄŠ∂† t in atTop, ‚ąÉ d‚āú : ‚ĄĚ, (DivisorSumSet t).HasDensity d‚āú ‚ąß
    42‚Üí    1 / Real.log t ^ c‚āÉ < d‚āú ‚ąß d‚āú < 1 / Real.log t ^ c‚āĄ := by
    43‚Üí  sorry
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üí/-
    47‚Üí**ErdŇĎs Problem 859**
    48‚ÜíThe density `d‚āú` of `DivisorSumSet (t : ‚Ąē)` is assymptotically equivalent to ` c‚āĀ / log (t) ^ c‚āā`
    49‚Üífor some positive constants `c‚āĀ` and `c‚āā`.
    50‚Üí-/
    51‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    52‚Üítheorem erdos_859 :
    53‚Üí    ‚ąÉ c‚āĀ > 0, ‚ąÉ c‚āā > (0 : ‚ĄĚ), ‚ąÉ d : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ, (‚ąÄ t > 0, (DivisorSumSet t).HasDensity (d t)) ‚ąß
    54‚Üí      (fun (t : ‚Ąē) ‚Ü¶ d t) ~[atTop] (fun t ‚Ü¶ c‚āĀ / Real.log t ^ c‚āā) := by
    55‚Üí  sorry
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üí/-
    58‚ÜíA case where we can easily calculate the density of `DivisorSumSet t` is that of `t=0`.
    59‚Üí-/
    60‚Üí@[category high_school, AMS 11]
    61‚Üílemma erdos_859.variants.trivial_case : DivisorSumSet 0 = Set.univ := by
    62‚Üí  simp [DivisorSumSet, Exists.intro ‚ąÖ]
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí/-
    65‚ÜíAn easy sanity check is to prove that for every natural number `t` the density `d‚āú` is
    66‚Üía positive number.
    67‚ÜíHint: investigate some multiplicative structure of `DivisorSumSet t`.
    68‚Üí-/
    69‚Üí@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    70‚Üílemma erdos_859.variants.positive_density (t : ‚Ąē) :
    71‚Üí    (DivisorSumSet t).HasPosDensity := by
    72‚Üí  sorry
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üíend Erdos859
    75‚Üí

    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 673
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/673](https://www.erdosproblems.com/673)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíLet $1 = d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)} = n$ be the divisors of $n$ and
    25‚Üí$$G(n) = \sum_{1 \leq i < \tau(n)} d_i / d_{i+1}.$$
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíIs it true that $G(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$? Can one prove an asymptotic
    28‚Üíformula for $\sum_{n \leq X} G(n)$?
    29‚Üí
    30‚ÜíErdŇĎs writes it is 'easy' to prove $(1/X) \sum_{n \leq X} G(n) \to \infty$.
    31‚Üí
    32‚ÜíTerence Tao observed that for any divisor $m \mid n$,
    33‚Üí$\tau(n/m) / m \leq G(n) \leq \tau(n)$,
    34‚Üíand hence for example $\tau(n)/4 \leq G(n) \leq \tau(n)$ for even $n$. It follows easily
    35‚Üíthat $G(n)$ grows on average and tends to infinity for almost all $n$.
    36‚Üí
    37‚ÜíErdŇĎs and Tenenbaum proved that $G(n)/\tau(n)$ has a continuous distribution
    38‚Üífunction.
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí[Er79e] ErdŇĎs, P., _Some unconventional problems in number theory_ (1979).
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí[Er82e] ErdŇĎs, P., _Some new problems and results in number theory_ (1982).
    43‚Üí-/
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üíopen Filter
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üínamespace Erdos673
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí/-- The sorted list of divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    50‚Üídef sortedDivisors (n : ‚Ąē) : List ‚Ąē :=
    51‚Üí  (Nat.divisors n).sort (¬∑ ‚Č§ ¬∑)
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí/-- $G(n) = \sum_{1 \leq i < \tau(n)} d_i / d_{i+1}$, where $d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)}$ are the
    54‚Üídivisors of $n$ in increasing order. This is the sum of ratios of consecutive
    55‚Üídivisors. -/
    56‚Üínoncomputable def erdosG (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    57‚Üí  let divs := sortedDivisors n
    58‚Üí  ((divs.zip divs.tail).map (fun p => (p.1 : ‚ĄĚ) / (p.2 : ‚ĄĚ))).sum
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí/-- The natural density of a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is zero. -/
    61‚Üídef HasNaturalDensityZero (A : Set ‚Ąē) [DecidablePred (¬∑ ‚ąą A)] : Prop :=
    62‚Üí  Tendsto
    63‚Üí    (fun N : ‚Ąē => (((Finset.range N).filter (¬∑ ‚ąą A)).card : ‚ĄĚ) / (N : ‚ĄĚ))
    64‚Üí    atTop (nhds 0)
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí/--
    67‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 673, Part 1 [Er79e, Er82e]:
    68‚Üí
    69‚Üí$G(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$, i.e., for every $M$, the set of $n$ with $G(n) \leq M$
    70‚Üíhas natural density zero.
    71‚Üí-/
    72‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    73‚Üítheorem erdos_673 :
    74‚Üí    answer(True) ‚ÜĒ ‚ąÄ M : ‚ĄĚ, HasNaturalDensityZero {n : ‚Ąē | erdosG n ‚Č§ M} := by
    75‚Üí  sorry
    76‚Üí
    77‚Üí/--
    78‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 673, Part 2 [Er79e, Er82e]:
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí$(1/X) \cdot \sum_{n \leq X} G(n) \to \infty$ as $X \to \infty$.
    81‚Üí-/
    82‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    83‚Üítheorem erdos_673.variants.average :
    84‚Üí    Tendsto
    85‚Üí      (fun X : ‚Ąē => (Finset.sum (Finset.range X) erdosG) / (X : ‚ĄĚ))
    86‚Üí      atTop atTop := by
    87‚Üí  sorry
    88‚Üí
    89‚Üíend Erdos673
    90‚Üí

    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 692
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/692](https://www.erdosproblems.com/692)
    23‚Üí
    24‚Üí[Er79e] ErdŇĎs, P., _Some unconventional problems in number theory_ (1979).
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí[Ob1] Oberwolfach problem session (1986).
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí[Ca25] Cambie, S., disproved the conjecture by exhibiting non-unimodality for $n = 3$.
    29‚Üí-/
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üíopen Finset Filter
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üínamespace Erdos692
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí/-- The number of divisors of $k$ that lie strictly between $n$ and $m$. -/
    36‚Üídef countDivisorsInOpenInterval (k n m : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    37‚Üí  ((Nat.divisors k).filter (fun d => n < d ‚ąß d < m)).card
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí/-- The proportion of positive integers up to $N$ having exactly one divisor
    40‚Üíin the open interval $(n, m)$. This approximates $\delta_1(n, m)$. -/
    41‚Üínoncomputable def delta1Approx (n m N : ‚Ąē) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    42‚Üí  (((Finset.range (N + 1)).filter
    43‚Üí    (fun k => 0 < k ‚ąß countDivisorsInOpenInterval k n m = 1)).card : ‚ĄĚ) / (N : ‚ĄĚ)
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí/-- The natural density $\delta_1(n, m)$ exists and equals $d$:
    46‚Üíthe limit of $|\{k \leq N : k \text{ has exactly one divisor in } (n, m)\}| / N$ equals $d$. -/
    47‚Üídef HasDelta1Density (n m : ‚Ąē) (d : ‚ĄĚ) : Prop :=
    48‚Üí  Filter.Tendsto (fun N => delta1Approx n m N) Filter.atTop (nhds d)
    49‚Üí
    50‚Üí/--
    51‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 692 [Er79e, Ob1] ‚ÄĒ DISPROVED
    52‚Üí
    53‚ÜíLet $\delta_1(n, m)$ be the natural density of the set of positive integers with exactly
    54‚Üíone divisor in the open interval $(n, m)$. ErdŇĎs asked (1986, Oberwolfach) whether,
    55‚Üífor fixed $n$, $\delta_1(n, m)$ is unimodal as a function of $m$ for $m > n + 1$.
    56‚Üí
    57‚ÜíCambie [Ca25] disproved this by showing it fails even for $n = 3$:
    58‚Üí$$\delta_1(3, 6) = 0.35, \quad \delta_1(3, 7) \approx 0.33, \quad \delta_1(3, 8) \approx 0.3619,$$
    59‚Üíexhibiting a "valley" at $m = 7$ that contradicts unimodality.
    60‚ÜíFurthermore, for fixed $n$, the sequence $\delta_1(n, m)$ has superpolynomially many
    61‚Üílocal maxima in $m$.
    62‚Üí
    63‚ÜíThe RHS formalizes unimodality: for every $n$ and $m_1 < m_2 < m_3$ (all $> n + 1$),
    64‚Üíthe density at $m_2$ is not a strict local minimum (i.e., $d_1 \le d_2$ or $d_3 \le d_2$).
    65‚ÜíThe answer is `False` because Cambie exhibited a counterexample.
    66‚Üí-/
    67‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    68‚Üítheorem erdos_692 : answer(False) ‚ÜĒ
    69‚Üí    ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÄ m‚āĀ m‚āā m‚āÉ : ‚Ąē,
    70‚Üí      n + 1 < m‚āĀ ‚Üí m‚āĀ < m‚āā ‚Üí m‚āā < m‚āÉ ‚Üí
    71‚Üí      ‚ąÄ d‚āĀ d‚āā d‚āÉ : ‚ĄĚ,
    72‚Üí        HasDelta1Density n m‚āĀ d‚āĀ ‚Üí
    73‚Üí        HasDelta1Density n m‚āā d‚āā ‚Üí
    74‚Üí        HasDelta1Density n m‚āÉ d‚āÉ ‚Üí
    75‚Üí        d‚āĀ ‚Č§ d‚āā ‚ą® d‚āÉ ‚Č§ d‚āā := by
    76‚Üí  sorry
    77‚Üí

    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíFor $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in [-1,1]$, define the Lagrange basis polynomials
    25‚Üí$$
    26‚Üí  \ell_k(x) = \prod_{i \neq k} \frac{x - x_i}{x_k - x_i},
    27‚Üí$$
    28‚Üíso that $\ell_k(x_k) = 1$ and $\ell_k(x_i) = 0$ for $i \neq k$.
    29‚Üí
    30‚ÜíLet $x_1, x_2, \ldots \in [-1,1]$ be an infinite sequence, and let
    31‚Üí$$
    32‚Üí  L_n(x) = \sum_{1 \leq k \leq n} |\ell_k(x)|,
    33‚Üí$$
    34‚Üíwhere each $\ell_k(x)$ is defined with respect to $x_1, \ldots, x_n$.
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí[Be31] Bernstein proved that the set of $x \in (-1,1)$ for which the limsup
    37‚Üícondition (Part 2) holds is everywhere dense.

    44‚Üí/-- The Lagrange basis polynomial $\ell_k(x)$ for nodes indexed by $\operatorname{Fin} n$.
    45‚Üí$\ell_k(x) = \prod_{i \neq k} \frac{x - x_i}{x_k - x_i}$ -/

    47‚Üí  ‚ąŹ i ‚ąą univ.filter (¬∑ ‚Č† k), (x - nodes i) / (nodes k - nodes i)
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí/-- The Lebesgue function: $L_n(x) = \sum_k |\ell_k(x)|$ -/
    50‚Üínoncomputable def lebesgueFunction {n : ‚Ąē} (nodes : Fin n ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ) (x : ‚ĄĚ) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    51‚Üí  ‚ąĎ k, |lagrangeBasis nodes k x|
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí/-- The first $n$ elements of an infinite sequence, viewed as $\operatorname{Fin} n \to \mathbb{R}$. -/
    54‚Üínoncomputable def firstN (seq : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ) (n : ‚Ąē) : Fin n ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ := fun i => seq i.val
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üí/-- $L_n(x)$: the Lebesgue function using the first $n$ points of the sequence. -/
    57‚Üínoncomputable def L (seq : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ) (n : ‚Ąē) (x : ‚ĄĚ) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    58‚Üí  lebesgueFunction (firstN seq n) x
    59‚Üí

    61‚Üídef ValidSeq (seq : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ) : Prop :=
    62‚Üí  Function.Injective seq ‚ąß ‚ąÄ i, seq i ‚ąą Set.Icc (-1 : ‚ĄĚ) 1
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí/--

    67‚ÜíFor any infinite sequence $x_1, x_2, \ldots \in [-1,1]$ of distinct points, must there
    68‚Üíexist $x \in (-1,1)$ and a constant $C$ such that $L_n(x) > \frac{2}{\pi} \log n - C$ for

    79‚Üí/--
    80‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1132 (Part 2):

    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1099](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1099)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíLet $1 = d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)} = n$ be the divisors of $n$, and for $\alpha > 1$ let
    25‚Üí$$h_\alpha(n) = \sum_i \left(\frac{d_{i+1}}{d_i} - 1\right)^\alpha.$$
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíIs it true that $\liminf_{n \to \infty} h_\alpha(n) \ll_\alpha 1$?
    28‚Üí
    29‚ÜíErdŇĎs [Er81h] remarks that $n!$ or $\mathrm{lcm}\{1, \ldots, n\}$ would be good candidates.
    30‚ÜíThe $\liminf$ is trivially $\geq 1$ (considering the term $i = 1$).
    31‚Üí
    32‚ÜíProved by Vose [Vo84] who constructed a specific sequence achieving bounded $h_\alpha(n)$.
    33‚ÜíIt remains open whether $n!$ or $\mathrm{lcm}\{1, \ldots, n\}$ satisfy this property.
    34‚Üí
    35‚ÜíThis resembles the function $G(n) = \sum_i d_i / d_{i+1}$ considered in problem \#673.
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí[Er81h] ErdŇĎs, P., _Some problems and results in number theory_.
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí[Vo84] Vose, M. D., _Integers with consecutive divisors in small ratio_, J. Number Theory (1984).
    40‚Üí-/

    46‚Üí/-- The sorted list of divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    47‚Üídef sortedDivisors (n : ‚Ąē) : List ‚Ąē :=
    48‚Üí  (Nat.divisors n).sort (¬∑ ‚Č§ ¬∑)
    49‚Üí
    50‚Üí/-- $h_\alpha(n) = \sum_i (d_{i+1}/d_i - 1)^\alpha$ where $d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)}$ are the
    51‚Üídivisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    52‚Üínoncomputable def hAlpha (őĪ : ‚ĄĚ) (n : ‚Ąē) : ‚ĄĚ :=
    53‚Üí  let ds := sortedDivisors n
    54‚Üí  ((ds.zip ds.tail).map (fun p => ((p.2 : ‚ĄĚ) / (p.1 : ‚ĄĚ) - 1) ^ őĪ)).sum
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üí/--
    57‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1099 (Proved by Vose [Vo84]):
    58‚Üí
    59‚ÜíFor every $\alpha > 1$, there exists a constant $C$ (depending on $\alpha$) such that
    60‚Üí$h_\alpha(n) \leq C$ for infinitely many $n$, i.e., $\liminf_{n \to \infty} h_\alpha(n)$
    61‚Üíis finite.
    62‚Üí
    63‚ÜíFormally: for every $\alpha > 1$, there exists $C$ such that for every $N$, there
    64‚Üíexists $n \geq N$ with $h_\alpha(n) \leq C$.
    65‚Üí-/

    68‚Üí    ‚ąÄ őĪ : ‚ĄĚ, őĪ > 1 ‚Üí ‚ąÉ C : ‚ĄĚ, ‚ąÄ N : ‚Ąē, ‚ąÉ n : ‚Ąē, n ‚Č• N ‚ąß hAlpha őĪ n ‚Č§ C := by
